Kaiser Foundation Health Plans Petition To Compel Arbitration And Motion To Stay Action; Declarations In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Mar-24-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT KAISER FOUNDATION HEALTH PLAN's Petition To Compel Arbitration And Motion To Stay Action.

No appearances. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant Kaiser Foundation Health Plan's petition to compel arbitration and stay is granted. As a Defendant shows that it entered into a Group Agreement and Evidence of Coverage with CalPERS that mandates the arbitration of "any claim" arising out of the Agreement. (Pet., Ex. A at 92.) As a CalPERS member and participant in its group health insurance policy (Compl., par. 4), Plaintiff is bound by that agreement. (Madden v. Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1976) 17 Cal.3d 699, 705-709 ["an agent or other fiduciary [such as the CalPERS Board] who contracts for medical treatment on behalf of his beneficiary retains the authority to enter into an agreement providing for arbitration of claims for medical malpractice."].) Such "contract provisions, as well as amendments to the contract, negotiated by the board within the scope of its authority as an agent, bind those employees who enroll under the contract." (Id. at 706.) CalPERS' compliance with the statutory and regulatory requirements governing the dissemination of informational materials to enrollees satisfies the requirements of the Knox-Keene Health Care Service Plan Act, including the arbitration disclosure requirements in Health & Safety Code section 1363.2. (Gov. Code section 22869.) For these reasons, Plaintiff's argument that she personally did not sign a document containing an arbitration provision is unavailing.

Plaintiff's cursory unconscionability argument likewise lacks merit. The Agreement is not a classic contract of adhesion. "Although plaintiff did not engage in the personal negotiation of the contract's terms, she and other public employees benefitted from representation by a board, composed in part of persons elected by the affected employees, which exerted its bargaining strength to secure medical protection for employees on more favorable terms than any employee could individually obtain." (Madden, 17 Cal.3d at 711.) In any event, both procedural and substantive unconscionability must be shown. (OTO, L.L.C. v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 125 ["Both procedural and substantive unconscionability must be shown for the defense to be established, but 'they need not be present in the same degree.'"].) Plaintiff makes no attempt to show that any provision of the Agreement is unfair or substantively unconscionable. (See also Viola v. Cal. Dept. of Managed Health Care (2005) 133 Cal.App.4th 299, 310 ["The fundamental problem with plaintiffs' theory is that the Legislature has expressly approved arbitration as a forum for resolution of disputes under health care service contracts"].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)
